“真的有必要吗？”霍尔斯特德公司(Halstead)的首席执行官黛安·M·拉米雷斯(Diane M.Ramirez)说，该公司在纽约地区有1000多名经纪人。“我对此深思熟虑，深思熟虑。展望未来，人们会想要挤进办公室吗？
当然，曼哈顿写字楼市场的消亡已经预测了几十年，特别是在9·11事件之后。11,2001，袭击事件。
写字楼的所有者，包括这座城市最大的两家房东沃纳多房地产信托公司(Vornado Realty Trust)和帝国房地产信托公司(Empire State Realty Trust)表示，他们相信，在这场危机之后，公司将比以往任何时候都更重视面对面的沟通。房东们说，考虑到自3月份开始停工以来，一些工人感到非常孤立，情况尤其如此。
有多少员工因为社交机会而实际上更喜欢呆在办公室里，这是一个未知的因素。
尼尔森(Nielsen)首席执行官大卫·肯尼(David Kenny)表示，该公司计划将其纽约办公室改造为团队会议场所，员工可能每周聚集在这里一到两次。
肯尼说，“如果你来这里，在办公桌前工作，你当然可以在家工作。”“我们有即将到期的租约，这绝对是推动这类决定的原因。”
巴克莱(Barclays)、摩根大通(JP Morgan Chase)和摩根士丹利(Morgan Stanley)都是银行业的一部分，银行业长期以来一直是香港经济的支柱，拥有2万多名员工。他们总共在纽约租赁了超过1000万平方英尺-大致是纳什维尔市中心所有的办公空间。
英国巴克莱银行(Barclays)首席执行官杰斯·斯特利(Jes Staley)说，“让7000人住在一栋大楼里的想法可能已经过去了。”
一位发言人说，公司正在研究最适合远程工作的工作，一些员工可能会被要求只有在需要的情况下才亲自到场。
摩根士丹利首席执行官詹姆斯·戈尔曼(James Gorman)拒绝了采访请求。但他告诉彭博社(Bloomberg)，该公司已经“证明了我们可以在没有足迹的情况下运营。这会告诉你很多关于人们需要在身体上呆在哪里的信息。“
摩根大通(JP Morgan Chase)在最近发给员工的一封电子邮件中表示，公司正在评估允许多少人返回。直到去年，摩根大通一直是纽约市最大的写字楼租户。超过18万名大通员工一直在家工作。
包括Facebook和谷歌在内的其他大公司已经将在家工作的政策延长到今年年底，这增加了一些公司可能永远不会回到办公室的可能性。Twitter在其位于曼哈顿切尔西社区的纽约办公室里有数百名员工。周二，该公司告诉所有员工，如果他们愿意，如果他们的位置允许的话，他们可以永远远程工作。
沃伦·巴菲特(Warren Buffett)是伯克希尔·哈撒韦公司(Berkshire Hathaway)的董事长，也是美国最著名的企业领导人之一。他预测，这场流行病将导致许多公司接受远程工作安排。巴菲特最近在他的年度投资者会议上说：“很多人都知道他们可以在家工作。”
纽约市经受住了一系列灾难和挫折-1918年的西班牙流感、大萧条、20世纪70年代的金融危机和2001年的恐怖袭击，并变得更加强大。每一次，人们都宣称这座城市将永远改变--9·11事件之后，谁会愿意在曼哈顿下城工作或生活呢？--但每次预言都落空了。
经济持续暴跌，失业率达到大萧条以来未曾见过的水平。许多公司陷入财务困境，可能会寻求缩小房地产规模，以此作为削减开支的一种方式。
更根本的是，如果社会距离仍然是公共健康的关键，公司如何才能安全地要求每一名工人回来？
“如果你在布鲁克林有250万人，为什么所有这些人每天都跑到曼哈顿工作，这是合理的，也是有效率的呢？”经营房地产公司Two Trees Management的杰德·瓦伦塔斯(Jed Walentas)说。“我们昨天就是这样做的。现在这是不理性的。“。
从地铁、公交车和通勤轨道的高峰期，到新建筑的建造，再到拐角车厢的生存，整个经济都是围绕着往返办公室的巨大人流而塑造的。餐馆、酒吧、杂货店和商店依靠工人生存。
房地产税约占纽约收入的三分之一，用于支付警察、垃圾清运和街道维修等基本服务费用。税收下降将加剧该市的金融危机，阻碍其复苏。
“我担心，收入越少，我们交的税和服务就会越少，就会形成恶性循环，”GFP Real Estate的联席首席执行官布莱恩·施泰因伍策尔(Brian Steinwurtzel)说。GFP Real Estate是纽约市最大的小型租户写字楼和零售楼的所有者和管理人。
曼哈顿的唐人街代表了上班族与周边社区之间的纽带。虽然唐人街吸引着游客，但许多餐厅和商店对工人的依赖即使不是更多，也是一样多，而这些工人通常每天都会从金融区以及附近的法院和市政大楼涌入。
54岁的简·李(Jan Lee)在该社区拥有两栋综合用途建筑，其中一栋是他祖父在1924年买下的。他说，“说我们不知道唐人街是否会在这里出现并不是什么戏剧性的事情。”
他的三个商业租户中的一个，一家化妆店，自1月份以来就没有支付过租金。他们中没有一家，包括两家以前很繁忙的餐厅，都没有支付5月份的租金。李先生有一笔大约25万美元的财产税账单，将于7月1日到期，他负担不起。
“我们损失了数百万美元，”他说，“还有数百万人在这里度过午餐时间的旅行。”
在唐人街的一家马来西亚和法国小酒馆AUX Epices，厨师兼老板梅卡胡(Mei Cahu)过去常常在午餐时招待多达50人，其中大部分是附近写字楼的员工。
“我经历了一段艰难的时期，我知道我会经历一段艰难的时期，”卡胡说。
就在几周前，房东、开发商和企业主还希望经济能在6月份基本重新开放。
但他们现在承认，现实情况是，夏末或初秋似乎更适合部分重新开放，而真正的重新开放-可能类似于熙熙攘攘的纽约-不会浮出水面，除非有疫苗或有效的治疗方法。
尽管如此，一些开发人员对工作环境的突然转变是否会以任何重大方式成为永久性的表示怀疑。
帝国地产信托公司(Empire State Realty Trust)的首席执行官安东尼·E·马尔金(Anthony E.Malkin)说，纽约的吸引力--多样化、受过教育的劳动力和大型产业，包括快速增长的科技行业--将推动经济反弹，并带动人们对办公空间的渴望。帝国地产信托公司拥有曼哈顿的帝国大厦和其他八处房产。
马尔金说，“今天人们赖以生存的社会联系的缺失是不可持续的。”“你能在家付账吗？”你能在家处理事情吗？是。但是你能在家里像一个团队一样工作吗？非常有挑战性。“。
商业地产公司世邦魏理仕(CBRE)纽约三州地区(New York Tri-State Region)的首席执行官玛丽·安·泰格(Mary Ann Tighe)表示，办公室无疑将发生变化，员工将混合远程工作。但员工仍然希望面对面互动。
“这不是办公室工作的本质，”提格说，她指的是在家工作的安排。
Vornado Realty Trust是该市最大的商业房东之一，其董事长Steven Roth本月在公司财报电话会议上表示，“我们认为在家办公不会成为损害写字楼需求和房产价值的趋势.。传统办公的社会化、协同化才是中奖门票。“
但出于安全或财务考虑-或者两者兼而有之-许多公司，无论大小，都在重新考虑工作的未来。
布鲁克林的一家小型软件开发商Small Planet表示，即使在该市重新开放后，其大约一半的员工也可能会继续远程工作。
该公司首席执行官加文·弗雷泽(Gavin Fraser)表示：“当我们走出隔离区时，世界将会不同，我们的习惯和使用办公空间的方式也将完全不同。”“如果你愿意的话，真的是采取了封锁措施才加速了这些趋势。”
note_en:
Before the coronavirus crisis, three of New York City’s largest commercial tenants — Barclays, JP Morgan Chase and Morgan Stanley — had tens of thousands of workers in towers across Manhattan. Now, as the city wrestles with when and how to reopen, executives at all three firms have decided that it is highly unlikely that all their workers will ever return to those buildings.
 The research firm Nielsen has arrived at a similar conclusion. Even after the crisis has passed, its 3,000 workers in the city will no longer need to be in the office full-time and can instead work from home most of the week.
 The real estate company Halstead has 32 branches across the city and region. But its chief executive, who now conducts business over video calls, is mulling reducing its footprint.
 Manhattan has the largest business district in the country, and its office towers have long been a symbol of the city’s global dominance. With hundreds of thousands of office workers, the commercial tenants have given rise to a vast ecosystem, from public transit to restaurants to shops. They have also funneled huge amounts of taxes into state and city coffers.
 But now, as the pandemic eases its grip, companies are considering not just how to safely bring back employees, but whether all of them need to come back at all. They were forced by the crisis to figure out how to function productively with workers operating from home — and realized unexpectedly that it was not all bad.
 If that’s the case, they are now wondering whether it’s worth continuing to spend as much money on Manhattan’s exorbitant commercial rents. They are also mindful that public health considerations might make the packed workplaces of the recent past less viable.
 “Is it really necessary?” said Diane M. Ramirez, the chief executive of Halstead, which has more than a thousand agents in the New York region. “I’m thinking long and hard about it. Looking forward, are people going to want to crowd into offices?’’
  Of course, the demise of the Manhattan office market has been predicted for decades, especially after the Sept. 11, 2001, attacks.
 Owners of office towers, including two of the largest landlords in the city, Vornado Realty Trust and Empire State Realty Trust, said they were confident that after this crisis, companies would value in-person communication more than ever. That’s especially the case given how isolated some workers have felt since the shutdown began in March, the landlords said.
 The number of workers who actually prefer to be in an office because of the opportunity for social interaction is an unknown factor.
  David Kenny, the chief executive at Nielsen, said the company plans to convert its New York offices to team meeting spaces where workers gather maybe once or twice a week.
 “If you are coming and working at your desk, you certainly could do that from home,” Mr. Kenny said. “We have leases that are coming due, and it’s absolutely driving those kinds of decisions.’’
 Barclays, JP Morgan Chase and Morgan Stanley are part of a banking industry that has long been a pillar of the city’s economy, with more than 20,000 employees. Collectively, they lease more than 10 million square feet in New York — roughly all the office space in downtown Nashville.
  Jes Staley, the chief executive of Barclays, the British bank,  said that “the notion of putting 7,000 people in a building may be a thing of the past.”
 The company is studying jobs that would be most adaptable to working remotely, a spokesman said, and some employees could be required to show up in person only on an as-needed basis.
 James Gorman, the Morgan Stanley chief executive, declined a request for an interview. But  he told Bloomberg that the company had “proven we can operate with no footprint. That tells you an enormous amount about where people need to be physically.”
 In a recent email to employees, JP Morgan Chase, which until last year had been the largest office tenant in New York City, said the company was reviewing how many people would be allowed to return. More than 180,000 Chase employees have been working from home.
 Other major companies, including Facebook and Google, have extended work-from-home policies through the end of the year, raising the prospect that some may never return to the office. Twitter, which has hundreds of employees in its New York office in the Chelsea neighborhood of Manhattan,  told all its employees on Tuesday that they could work remotely forever if they want to and if their position allows for it.
 Warren Buffett, the chairman of Berkshire Hathaway and one of the country’s most prominent corporate leaders, predicted that the pandemic would lead many companies to embrace remote working arrangements. “A lot of people have learned that they can work at home,” Mr. Buffett said recently during his annual investors meeting.
  New York City has withstood and emerged stronger from a number of catastrophes and setbacks — the 1918 Spanish Flu, the Great Depression, the 1970s financial crisis and the 2001 terrorist attacks. Each time, people proclaimed the city would forever change — after 9/11, who would want to work or live in Lower Manhattan? — but each time the prognostications fizzled.
 The economy is in a sustained nosedive, with unemployment reaching levels not seen since the Great Depression. Many companies are in financial trouble and may look to shrink their real estate as a way to cut expenses.
 More fundamentally, if social distancing remains a key to public health, how can companies safely ask every worker to come back?
 “If you got two and a half million people in Brooklyn, why is it rational or efficient for all those people to schlep into Manhattan and work every day?” said Jed Walentas, who runs the real estate company Two Trees Management. “That’s how we used to do it yesterday. It’s not rational now.”
 Entire economies were molded around the vast flow of people to and from offices, from the rush-hour schedules of subways, buses and commuter rails to the construction of new buildings to the survival of corner bodegas. Restaurants, bars, grocery stores and shops depend on workers for their survival.
  Real estate taxes provide about a third of New York’s revenue, helping pay for basic services like the police, trash pickup and street repairs. Falling tax revenue would worsen the city’s financial crisis and hinder its recovery.
 “I get worried that the less money that is coming in, then we can pay less in taxes and less in services, and it becomes a vicious cycle,” said Brian Steinwurtzel, the co-chief executive at GFP Real Estate, the largest owner and manager of small tenant office and retail buildings in the city.
 Chinatown in Manhattan typifies the bond between office workers and surrounding neighborhoods. While Chinatown attracts tourists, many restaurants and stores rely just as much if not more on workers that typically pour in every day from the Financial District and nearby courthouses and municipal buildings.
 “It is not dramatic to say that we don’t know if Chinatown is going to be here when we come out of this,” said Jan Lee, 54, who owns two mixed-use buildings in the neighborhood, including one that his grandfather bought in 1924.
 One of his three commercial tenants, a makeup store, has not paid rent since January. None of them, including two formerly busy restaurants, have paid May rent. Mr. Lee has a roughly $250,000 property tax bill due on July 1 that he cannot afford to pay.
 “We have lost millions of dollars,’’ he said, “and millions of trips that people were taking to spend their lunch hour here.”
 At Aux Epices, a Malaysian and French bistro in Chinatown, Mei Cahu, the chef and owner, used to serve up to 50 people at lunch, mostly workers from nearby office buildings.
  “I have had a hard time, and I know I’ll have a hard time,” Ms. Cahu said.
 Landlords, developers and business owners were hopeful just a few weeks ago that the economy could largely reopen in June.
 But the reality, they now concede, is that late summer or early fall seems more realistic for a partial reopening, while a true reopening — something that might resemble a bustling New York — will not surface until there is a vaccine or effective therapeutics.
 Still, some developers are dubious that the sudden shift in work environments will become permanent in any significant way.
 Anthony E. Malkin, the chief executive of Empire State Realty Trust, the owner of the Empire State Building and eight other properties in Manhattan, said New York’s appeal — a diverse and educated work force and large industries, including a fast-growing technology sector — would drive an economic rebound and a desire for office space.
 “The absence of social contact through which people are living today is not sustainable,” Mr. Malkin said. “Can you pay the bills from home? Can you process things from home? Yes. But can you work as a team from home? Very challenging.”
  Mary Ann Tighe, the chief executive of CBRE’s New York Tri-State Region, the commercial real estate firm, said offices will undoubtedly change, with a mix of employees working remotely. But workers will still want to interact face to face.
 “This isn’t the nature of office work,” Ms. Tighe said, referring to work-from-home arrangements.
 Steven Roth, chairman of Vornado Realty Trust, one of the largest commercial landlords in the city,  said on a company earnings call this month, “We do not believe working from home will become a trend that will impair office demand and property values. The socialization and collaboration of the traditional office is the winning ticket.”
 But driven by safety or financial considerations — or both — many companies, big and small, are rethinking the future of work.
 Small Planet, a small software developer in Brooklyn, said about half its work force is likely to continue working remotely even after the city reopens.
 “The world is going to be different when we come out of quarantine, and our habits and how we use office space will absolutely be different,” said Gavin Fraser, the company’s chief executive. “It really took the lockdown, if you will, to accelerate those trends.”
title: 如果在家工作成为常态，曼哈顿将面临清算
title_en: Manhattan Faces a Reckoning If Working from Home Becomes the Norm

note:
一个月前，苹果(Apple)和谷歌(Google)介入，为目前正在开发的联系人追踪应用程序带来了技术严谨和隐私保护。正如我之前报道的那样，不幸的事实是，尽管这两家科技巨头在智能手机市场占据主导地位，但它们无法解决这些应用程序的普及问题，这对它们的有效性产生了实质性影响。
在这两家科技巨头取得控制权后，随之而来的隐私风暴也造成了比好事更大的伤害-进一步影响了用户的接受，并塑造了人们的态度。而现在，拥有最早这类应用程序的新开发，对于其他这些应用程序未来可能取得成功来说，是一个重大挫折。
快速回顾-国家卫生机构可以开发使用蓝牙无线电技术的应用程序，以记录在附近检测到其他安装了相同应用程序的手机的时间。当用户出现症状或冠状病毒检测呈阳性时，可以向前一两周内与其关系密切的所有人发送警报，并提供下一步行动的建议。其想法是，这可以帮助解除封锁，让各国重新开始工作和娱乐。
苹果和谷歌的联合倡议将不会开发应用程序。相反，他们的操作系统更新将使技术在后台更高效地工作。这些应用程序将不得不为这种便利付出代价，那就是遵守严格的隐私规则。这本身就造成了问题-苹果和谷歌希望所有数据只保存在设备上，没有中央数据存储库或工具。英国、法国和其他地方的卫生服务机构声称，这阻止了对感染火锅和感染率的分析。科技巨头还禁止这些应用程序记录位置ping。
这突显了接触者追踪的隐私和有效性之间的二分法。你越是淡化系统来保护个人数据，你推动有效结果的机会就越小。你不能两全其美--要么想要控制感染率，要么想要保护数据。
有关隐私的辩论加剧了人们的担忧，即这种由政府开发和部署的应用程序可能会成为一种国家级的监控形式。这是不现实的，但它仍然推动了抗议活动，这反过来又强化了苹果和谷歌对数据保护的态度。
这一切都降低了可能的购买量--此外，还有大量没有最新苹果或谷歌操作系统的旧手机，或者范围广泛的中国手机无法使用这项技术。数据建模人员曾表示，有效性需要任何国家高达80%的智能手机用户安装这款应用，并遵守其说明。这种接受和坚持需要持续数月。
如果没有一定程度的强制性要求为了工作或旅行而使用应用程序，没有一个国家会达到80%的安装基数。新加坡通过TraceTogether开始推动这类蓝牙应用程序的发展，目前只覆盖了大约20%-25%的人口。其他人也看到了同样的情况。现实地说，30%的用户群将是一个坚实的成就，但这远远不够。
因此，来自新加坡的最新消息具有严重的讽刺意味。受到第二波感染的打击，随着其TraceTogether应用程序的使用率徘徊在太低的水平，不足以产生足够的影响，这个城市国家已经进入了下一个阶段，启动了一个新的接触者追踪监控项目，这将让那些反对使用这些技术的团体感到恐惧。
从今天起，也就是5月12日，新加坡的SafeEntry全面投入运营。任何访问范围广泛的地点的人都需要使用国家形式的ID或通过扫描智能手机上的二维码来办理登机手续。这些地点包括工作场所、学校、商店、酒店和医疗设施。未能为游客或客户办理登机手续的企业将面临处罚风险。甚至还采取了一些举措，在出租车内和其他交通系统上纳入了登机手续。
“在个人可能长时间接近或封闭空间的地方，”政府解释说，“或交通流量较大的地方，将强制进行部署。”员工和访客应该使用SafeEntry对工作场所和其他场所进行签到和签到，以帮助我们的接触追踪器建立集群链接和传输链。“。
这是有效的接触者追踪，但它是侵入性的，并建立了一个数据库，确实引发了真正的监视和隐私问题。这也是对那些有效追踪接触者的承认，它利用了人工追踪、闭路电视监控、电话位置记录和深入访谈。没有任何地方可以单独运行选择加入的数字应用程序。记录的安全入口数据包括姓名、身份证、电话号码和从各个位置进入(最好是离开)的时间。
如果基于手机的联系人追踪要有效，世界各国政府将需要解决低使用率问题，并在整个夏季和明年冬天保持使用率，敦促用户忽略错误警报，并在接到指示时隔离或寻求测试。在大城市，这几乎是不可能的。这些政府将被集中数据的前景所吸引，这些数据可以帮助确定需要在哪里实施更严格的措施，如局部封锁。如果没有强制使用，没有与端到端跟踪和测试计划的链接，以及对未能遵守的惩罚，这些应用程序将不会有效。
对于应用程序可以独立于所有其他措施运行的想法来说，新加坡的SafeEntry系统是一个重大挫折，特别是来自相对合规的新加坡，那里的使用率几乎没有好于西方的预期。那些生活在欧洲和北美的人会认为新加坡最新的追踪措施不太可能在他们自己的国家实施，但快进到几周后，感染会进一步激增，我们可能会感到惊讶。
note_en:
A month ago, Apple and Google  stepped in to bring technical rigour and privacy safeguards to the wave of contact-tracing apps now under development. As I’ve reported before, the unfortunate truth, though, is that the two tech giants, despite their dominance of the smartphone market,  cannot resolve the take-up problem with these apps, with material implications for their effectiveness.
  In the aftermath of the two tech giants taking control, the privacy storm that has ensued has also caused more harm than good—impacting take-up further and shaping attitudes. And now a new development with the earliest such app is a major setback for the likely success of the rest of these apps moving forward.
  Quick recap—national health agencies can develop apps that use Bluetooth radio technology to record when other phones with the same app are detected nearby. When a user either shows symptoms or tests positive for COVID-19, alerts can be sent to all those who were in close proximity over the preceding week or two, along with advice on what to do next. The idea is that this can help unlock lockdowns and send countries back to work and play.
       Apple and Google’s joint initiative will  not develop apps. Instead, their operating system updates will make the tech work more efficiently in the background. The price those apps will have to pay for this convenience is adhering to strict privacy rules. That has itself caused issues—Apple and Google want all data to be held only on devices, with no central data repositories or tools. Health services in the U.K., France and elsewhere claim this prevents analysis of infection hotpots and rates. The tech giants have also banned location pings to be recorded by these apps.
    This has highlighted the dichotomy between privacy and efficacy with contact-tracing. The more you water down the system to protect an individual’s data, the less chance you have of driving effective outcomes. You can’t have it both ways—either you want to stem infection rates or you want to safeguard data.
  The privacy debate has ramped up fears that this type of app, developed and deployed by governments, might become a form of national-level surveillance. This isn’t realistic, but it has still promoted protests, which in turn has hardened Apple and Google’s attitude to data protection.
  This all reduces likely take-up— in addition to the numbers of older phones or phones without an up-to-date Apple or Google OS or a wide range of Chinese phones can't get the tech. Data modellers have said that effectiveness needs as many as 80% of smartphone owners in any country to install the app and adhere to its instructions. This take-up and adherence needs to run for many months.
  Without some level of mandatory requirement to use an app in order to work or travel, without penalties, no country will attain the 80% install base. Singapore started the push towards these types of Bluetooth apps with TraceTogether, and has only reached around 20-25% of its population. Others are seeing the same. Realistically, a 30% install base across a population would be a solid achievement, and that’s nowhere close to being enough.
  And so there’s a serious irony to the latest news to come from Singapore. Hit by a second wave of infections and with the take-up of its TraceTogether app hovering at too low a level to make enough of a difference, the city state has moved to the next level, launching a new contact-tracing surveillance program that will strike fear into those groups campaigning against the use of these technologies.
  From today, May 12, Singapore’s  SafeEntry goes fully operational. Anyone visiting a wide range of locations will need to check-in with either a national form of ID or by scanning a QR code on their smartphones. Those locations include workplaces, schools, stores, hotels and healthcare facilities. Businesses failing to check-in visitors or customers risk penalties. There are even moves to include  check-in processes inside taxis and on other transit systems.
  “D eployment will be made mandatory for places where individuals are likely to be in close proximity for prolonged periods or in enclosed spaces,”  explains the government , “or where there is higher traffic... Employees and visitors should check-in and check-out of workplaces and other venues using SafeEntry to help our contact tracers establish cluster links and transmission chains.”
       This  is  effective contact-tracing, but it’s invasive and builds a database that does trigger genuine surveillance and privacy concerns. It is also an acknowledgement that where contact-tracing has been effective, it has made use of manual tracing, CCTV monitoring, phone location records and in-depth interviews. Nowhere have opt-in digital apps worked alone. Recorded SafeEntry data includes names, IDs, phone numbers and times of entry (and ideally exit) from locations.
  If phone-based contact-tracing is to be effective, governments worldwide will need to address low take-up rates and maintain usage through the summer and into next winter, urging users to ignore false alerts and isolate or seek testing whenever instructed. In major cities this will be near impossible. Those same governments will be tantalized with the prospect of centralized data that can help determine where more stringent measures such as localized lockdowns need to be put into effect. Without mandatory usage, linkage to an end-to-end track and test program and penalties for failure to adhere, the apps will not be effective.
  Singapore’s SafeEntry system is a major setback for the idea that apps can operate independently of all those other measures, especially coming from relatively compliant Singapore, where take-up has been little better than is expected in the West. Those living in Europe and North America will assume Singapore’s latest tracking measures are unlikely in their own countries, but fast forward a few weeks to further infection spikes and we may be surprised.
title: 忘了苹果和谷歌吧-联系人追踪应用程序刚刚遭受了严重的新打击
title_en: Forget Apple and Google – Contact Tracing Apps Just Dealt Serious New Blow

note:
在司法部有争议地撤销对迈克·弗林(Mike Flynn)的指控几天后，参议院共和党多数党领袖米奇·麦康奈尔(Mitch McConnell)将在本周就延长2001年“爱国者法案”(Patriot Act)进行投票时，扩大高度政治化的司法部的监视权力。
在纠正唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统及其盟友所称的联邦调查局(FBI)因与克里姆林宫串通而进行的“政治迫害”的掩护下，麦康奈尔将通过“爱国者法案”(Patriot Act)的一项修正案，明确允许联邦调查局在未经授权的情况下收集美国人的网络浏览和搜索历史记录。在另一项修正案中，麦康奈尔还提议让司法部长了解联邦调查局(FBI)向秘密外国情报监视法案(FISA)法院提交的监视报告的“准确性和完整性”。周一流传的修正案版本被分享给了每日野兽报。
综上所述，隐私权倡导者认为麦康奈尔的举动是对司法部长比尔·巴尔(Bill Barr)根据FISA的权力的惊人扩大。FISA是一个已有40年历史的程序，已经将司法部长置于国家安全监控的中心。他们也注意到，麦康奈尔正在加强巴尔对政治候选人的监控，同时扩大对其他所有美国人的监控。一位隐私权活动家称麦康奈尔的努力是“我所见过的破坏监控改革的两次最愤世嫉俗的尝试。”
参议员罗恩·怀登(D-OR)表示，一直深度参与特朗普感兴趣的调查的巴尔可能会授权对政治对手进行调查，然后这可能会解锁麦康奈尔希望授予联邦调查局的互联网间谍权力。
怀登说：“根据麦康奈尔修正案，巴尔可以在没有搜查令的情况下查看任何美国人的网络浏览历史，包括记者、政客和政治对手，只需说这与调查有关。”怀登一直试图禁止对此类记录进行未经授权的监控。
恢复“爱国者法案”(Patriot Act)到期条款的投票最快可能在周二或周三进行。爱国者法案是麦康奈尔修正案的载体。
巴尔因代表特朗普腐败他的办公室而受到前司法部官员的严厉批评，从去年的罗伯特·穆勒(Robert Mueller)开始。周日，美国国务院前高级国家安全官员玛丽·麦考德(Mary McCord)指责巴尔的盟友蒂莫西·谢伊(Timothy Shea)在撤销指控的案情摘要中歪曲了她对弗林调查的立场。一天后，前罗杰·斯通(Roger Stone)检察官、公共腐败专家乔纳森·克拉维斯(Jonathan Kravis)写道，巴尔“直接干预”以使总统的同僚受益，“背叛了法治”。
麦康奈尔的修正案阻止联邦调查局寻求美国人进行的网络浏览和搜索的“内容”。但它明确允许未经授权收集“互联网网站浏览记录或互联网搜索历史记录”。巴尔和其他司法部长批准了进行此类监控的指导方针。
怀登和来自蒙大拿州的共和党同事史蒂夫·戴恩斯(Steve Daines)一直在推动限制情报机构未经授权的网络浏览数据收集。怀登认为麦康奈尔的修正案令人震惊。
怀登告诉“每日野兽报”：“麦康奈尔修正案中提到的‘内容’是没有意义的，因为它对网络浏览的适用从未在法庭上得到解决。”“这只是对巴尔的邀请，让他对法律进行更多的秘密解释，这导致了一次又一次的滥用。”这指的是从2006年到2015年，美国国家安全局和司法部是如何将大量收集美国人电话记录的行为强加给“爱国者法案”的一部分。
这并不是麦康奈尔通过爱国者法案投票对FISA做出改变的限制。
麦康奈尔将要求巴尔对联邦调查局(FBI)提交的FISA文件进行年度审查，以确保“准确性或完整性”，这是指FBI官员因重新加强对特朗普竞选助手卡特·佩奇(Carter Page)的监控而隐瞒的内容。巴尔和他的继任者每年4月都会向国会司法和情报委员会提交一份关于他的调查结果的报告。
考虑到司法部监察长迈克尔·霍洛维茨(Michael Horowitz)最近在FBI的FISA申请中发现了广泛的缺陷，远远超出了与特朗普或他的盟友相关的缺陷，对FBI的额外监督是有好处的。
但公民自由主义参议员迈克·李(R-UT)和帕特里克·莱希(D-VT)提出了一项相互竞争的提案，将那些联邦调查局监控申请的监督和审查交由相对中立的FISA法院及其之友负责，后者是一名律师，对政府的监控提交提出质疑。他们，而不是司法部长，将有机会审查关于拟议的监视目标的无罪证据。
麦康奈尔还会阻止“之友”权威的扩大。Lee和Leahy提议让对抗性律师参与拟议中的监视“国内宗教或政治组织”、“国内公职人员或政治候选人”或他们的工作人员，或新闻媒体。相反，麦康奈尔只在“针对美国人的联邦办公室竞选活动或申请只依赖于”外国特工注册法“的条款作为刑事论断的情况下，才授权在法院审理”之友“。”
美国公民自由联盟(ACLU)的尼玛·辛格·朱利安尼(Neema Singh Giuliani)表示，“当针对政治候选人时，创建一个参与的朋友是很奇怪的，但我们不会在涉及宗教组织、国内新闻媒体或面临新的或重大民权问题的普通个人的案件中提供同样的监督。”很难看到这项修正案并得出结论，它的目的不仅是为了真正解决卡特·佩奇报告暴露的问题，也是为了解决随后的IG审计。“
“麦康奈尔实际上是在试图采取为媒体和宗教团体设计的隐私保护措施，而不是只给政客和被怀疑是外国特工的人。他还试图将对互联网和搜索历史的未经授权的监控偷偷溜进一项声称禁止的修正案中，“活动团体Demand Progress的高级政策委员会肖恩·维特卡(Sean Vitka)补充道。“这是我见过的破坏监控改革的最愤世嫉俗的两次尝试，它们可能会让”爱国者法案“(Patriot Act)的重新授权变得更糟，此前这一过程迄今成功阻止了任何国会议员修改基本法案。”
2001年“爱国者法案”(Patriot Act)第215条要求记录提供商交出与正在进行的调查“相关”的“有形物品”-麦康奈尔的修正案将扩大到网络浏览和搜索历史记录。它和爱国者法案的其他条款上个月到期，因为麦康奈尔无法达成协议，通过众议院批准的爱国者重新授权，这是许多隐私权倡导者憎恨的。
朱利安尼说，麦康奈尔的修正案“可以解读为某种类型的收集(互联网记录)是适当的，并将其留给司法部的领导层。”但我们的目标不是让司法部来决定，不管你在哪个政府，而是要让人感觉到，搜索和浏览历史记录在215岁以下是收集不到的。
麦康奈尔的参议院办公室没有立即回复记者的置评请求。司法部的代表也没有。
周一，包括全国有色人种协进会(NAACP)和自由工作(FreedomWorks)在内的左翼和右翼民权团体联盟向立法者分发了一封信，敦促按照李、莱希和怀登的提议限制监控。这两个组织一致认为，霍洛维茨的报告暴露了联邦调查局监视实践中的“系统性缺陷”。
怀登说：“麦康奈尔修正案赋予司法部长巴尔巨大的监控权，而此时此刻，他正在加速司法部的腐败，以满足唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)的政治突发奇想。”
note_en:
Days after the Justice Department controversially  dropped charges against Mike Flynn, Senate GOP Majority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-KY) is set to expand a highly politicized Justice Department’s surveillance authority during a vote this week to renew the 2001 PATRIOT Act.
 Under cover of redressing what President Donald Trump and his allies call the FBI’s “witch hunt” over collusion with the Kremlin, McConnell, via an amendment to the PATRIOT Act, will expressly permit the FBI to warrantlessly collect records on Americans’ web browsing and search histories. In a different amendment, McConnell also proposes giving the attorney general visibility into the “accuracy and completeness” of FBI surveillance submissions to the secret Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) Court. Versions of the amendments circulating Monday were shared with The Daily Beast.
 Taken together, privacy advocates consider McConnell’s moves an alarming expansion of Attorney General Bill Barr’s powers under FISA, a four-decade-old process that already places the attorney general at the center of national-security surveillance. It also doesn’t escape their notice that McConnell is increasing Barr’s oversight of surveillance on political candidates while expanding surveillance authorities on every other American. One privacy activist called McConnell’s efforts “two of the most cynical attempts to undermine surveillance reform I've ever seen.”
  Sen. Ron Wyden (D-OR) said that Barr, who has been deeply involved in investigations of interest to Trump, could authorize an investigation into a political rival, which could then unlock the internet-spying powers McConnell wants to grant the FBI.
 “Under the McConnell amendment, Barr gets to look through the web browsing history of any American—including journalists, politicians, and political rivals—without a warrant, just by saying it is relevant to an investigation,” said Wyden, who has been trying to ban warrantless surveillance on such records.
  A vote to restore expired provisions of the Patriot Act, the vehicle for McConnell’s amendments, could come as soon as Tuesday or Wednesday.
 Barr has come under withering criticism from ex-Justice Department officials for corrupting his office on Trump’s behalf,  starting with Robert Mueller last year. On Sunday, Mary McCord, a former senior department national-security official, accused Barr ally Timothy Shea of  misrepresenting her position on the Flynn investigation in his brief for dropping the charges. A day later, former Roger Stone prosecutor Jonathan Kravis, a  public-corruption expert, wrote that Barr had “betray[ed] the rule of law” by “directly intervenin[ing] to benefit the president’s associates.”
 McConnell’s amendment blocks the FBI from seeking the “content” of web browsing and searching conducted by Americans. But it explicitly permits the warrantless collection of “Internet website browsing records or internet search history records.” Barr and other attorneys general approve guidelines for conducting such surveillance.
 Wyden and GOP colleague Steve Daines of Montana have been pushing to restrict warrantless web-browsing data collection by the intelligence agencies. Wyden considers McConnell’s amendment egregious.
 “The reference to ‘content’ in the McConnell amendment is meaningless, since its application to web browsing has never been settled in the courts,” Wyden told The Daily Beast. “That’s just an invitation to Barr to engage in more secret interpretations of the law, which have led to abuses again and again.” That’s a reference to how the NSA and Justice Department, from 2006 until 2015, shoehorned the  bulk collection of Americans’ phone records into  part of the Patriot Act.
 It’s not the limit of McConnell’s changes to FISA through the Patriot Act vote.
 McConnell would mandate that Barr perform an annual review of the FBI’s FISA submissions for “accuracy or completeness,” speaking to the now-documented withholdings FBI officials made for re-upping surveillance on  Trump campaign aide Carter Page. Barr and his successors would present the congressional committees on judiciary and intelligence with a report on his findings each April.
 Additional oversight of the FBI has merit to it, considering that Justice Department inspector general Michael Horowitz recently found  widespread flaws in the FBI’s FISA applications, far beyond those relevant to Trump or his allies.
 But there’s a competing proposal, from the civil libertarian Senators Mike Lee (R-UT) and Patrick Leahy (D-VT), that puts oversight and review of those FBI surveillance applications in the hands of the comparatively neutral FISA Court and its amicus, an attorney who challenges the government’s surveillance submissions. They, rather than the attorney general, would get to review exculpatory evidence on a proposed surveillance target.
 McConnell would also block expansion of the amicus’ authority. Lee and Leahy propose to involve the adversarial attorney in proposed surveillance on “a domestic religious or political organization”; a “domestic public official or political candidate” or their staff; or the news media. McConnell instead authorizes an amicus before the court only in cases that “targe[t] a campaign for Federal office or an application that targets a United States person when the application relies for its criminal predicate on only the provisions of the Foreign Agents Registration Act.”
  Neema Singh Giuliani of the ACLU said it was bizarre to “create an amicus to participate when targeting political candidates, but we’re not going to provide that same oversight in cases involving religious organizations, domestic news media or everyday individuals who are facing new or significant civil rights concerns. It’s hard to look at that amendment and conclude it’s intended to really address not just problems exposed by the Carter Page report but the subsequent IG audit.”
 “McConnell is literally trying to take a privacy safeguard designed for the press and religious groups and instead give it only to politicians and people suspected of being foreign agents. He's also trying to sneak warrantless surveillance of internet and search histories into an amendment that claims to prohibit it,” added Sean Vitka, the senior policy council with activist group Demand Progress. “These are two of the most cynical attempts to undermine surveillance reform I've ever seen, and they threaten to make a Patriot Act reauthorization even worse, after a process that has so far successfully prevented any member of Congress from fixing the underlying bill.”
 Section 215 of the 2001 Patriot Act mandates records providers turn over “tangible things” “relevant” to an ongoing investigation–which McConnell’s amendment would extend to web-browsing and search-history records. It and other provisions of the Patriot Act  expired last month after McConnell couldn’t reach an agreement to pass a House-approved Patriot reauthorization that  many privacy advocates hated.
 Giuliani said McConnell’s amendment “can be read that some type of collection [on internet records] is appropriate, and leaves it up to leadership at the Justice Department. But the goal is not to leave this up to a DOJ determination, regardless of what administration you’re in, but giving the sense that search and browsing history is something you can't collect under 215.”
 McConnell’s Senate office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Neither did a Justice Department representative.
 On Monday, a coalition of left and right civil-rights groups, including the NAACP and FreedomWorks,  circulated a letter to lawmakers urging restraints on surveillance in line with what Lee, Leahy, and Wyden are proposing. The groups agreed that Horowitz’s report laid bare “systemic deficiencies” within the FBI’s surveillance practices.
 “The McConnell amendment hands vast surveillance powers to Attorney General Barr at a moment when he is accelerating the corruption of the Department of Justice to serve Donald Trump’s political whims,” said Wyden.
title: 米奇·麦康奈尔(Mitch McConnell)采取行动，允许未经授权收集浏览和搜索历史记录
title_en: Mitch McConnell Moves to Allow Warrantless Collection of Browsing&Search History
